  • A non-static inner class holding its outer instance.
  • Mutating a key's hash while it's in a HashMap.
tip: GC frees UNREACHABLE objects, not USELESS ones. Anything a static field points at lives for the life of the JVM.
code:
// classic leak — nothing is ever removed
private static final Map<String, User> CACHE = new HashMap<>();

question: What kinds of OutOfMemoryError are there?
answer:
• Java heap space — too many live objects: a leak, or the heap is too small.
  • GC overhead limit exceeded — >98% of time in GC reclaiming <2%. Effectively a leak.
  • Metaspace — too many loaded classes (classic in app servers that redeploy).
  • unable to create new native thread — OS thread limit hit.
tip: Run with -XX:+HeapDumpOnOutOfMemoryError, then open the dump in Eclipse MAT — it names the leaking object graph.
code:
java -Xmx2g -XX:+HeapDumpOnOutOfMemoryError \
     -XX:HeapDumpPath=/tmp/dump.hprof -jar app.jar

question: What causes StackOverflowError?
answer:
The thread's stack is exhausted — almost always unbounded recursion (a missing or wrong base case), or occasionally mutual recursion or an accidental infinite toString()/equals() loop.

Java has no tail-call optimization, so deep recursion doesn't get flattened — convert it to a loop.
tip: Java has NO tail-call optimization — recursion that's fine in Scala or Haskell will overflow here.
code: int bad(int n) { return bad(n - 1); }   // no base case

question: How does class loading work?
answer:
Three phases: Loading (read the bytecode), Linking (verify → prepare → resolve), Initialization (run static blocks/fields).

Loaders form a chain — Bootstrap → Platform → Application — using parent delegation: each asks its parent first, so nobody can substitute a fake java.lang.String.
tip: Parent delegation is a security feature: core JDK classes always load from the bootstrap loader, so they can't be spoofed.

question: ClassNotFoundException vs NoClassDefFoundError.
answer:
ClassNotFoundException (checked) — an explicit dynamic lookup like Class.forName("x") found nothing on the classpath.

NoClassDefFoundError (an Error) — the class was present at compile time but is missing at runtime, or it failed its static initialization earlier and the JVM now refuses to use it.
tip: NoClassDefFoundError often means the class's static initializer threw — look for an earlier ExceptionInInitializerError, not a missing jar.

question: What is the JIT compiler?
answer:
The JVM starts by interpreting bytecode, profiles what actually runs, then the JIT compiles hot methods to native code — applying inlining, loop unrolling, and escape analysis.

Because it optimizes on real runtime data, long-running Java can rival C++ — but only after warm-up.
tip: JIT warm-up is why naive microbenchmarks lie — use JMH, which warms up before measuring.

question: What are the headline features of the recent LTS releases?
answer:
• Java 8 — lambdas, Streams, Optional, new date/time API.
  • Java 11 — var in lambdas, HttpClient, String.isBlank/lines/repeat.
  • Java 17 — records, sealed classes, switch expressions, text blocks, pattern-matching instanceof.
  • Java 21 — virtual threads, pattern matching for switch, record patterns, sequenced collections.
tip: LTS releases are 8, 11, 17, 21 — those are the ones companies actually run. Java 21's headline is virtual threads.

question: Why did Java 8 replace Date and Calendar?
answer:
The old API was mutable (so not thread-safe), had a 0-based month, and SimpleDateFormat was famously not thread-safe.

java.time is immutable and clear: LocalDate (no time), LocalDateTime (no zone), ZonedDateTime (zone-aware), Instant (a UTC timestamp), Duration/Period.
tip: A static SimpleDateFormat shared across threads silently corrupts dates — java.time's DateTimeFormatter is immutable and safe.

question: What is reflection and what is it used for?
answer:
Inspecting and invoking classes, fields, and methods at runtime by name. It's how Spring does dependency injection, Jackson maps JSON, JUnit finds @Test, and Hibernate populates entities.

Costs: no compile-time safety, slower than direct calls, and it can break encapsulation.
tip: You rarely write reflection — but every framework you use is built on it. That's the answer interviewers want.

question: What are annotations, and how do you write one?
answer:
Metadata attached to code, read by the compiler or by frameworks at runtime via reflection.

To be visible at runtime an annotation needs @Retention(RUNTIME); @Target restricts where it can be applied.
tip: The default retention is CLASS, not RUNTIME — forget @Retention(RUNTIME) and your framework will never see the annotation.

question: What is the module system (Java 9)?
answer:
JPMS adds strong encapsulation above the package level. A module-info.java declares what the module exports and what it requires — so a public class in a non-exported package is genuinely inaccessible.

It also allows jlink to build a trimmed runtime with only the modules you use.
tip: Before modules, 'public' meant public to the entire world. Modules finally let a library hide its internals.

question: Strong, weak, soft and phantom references.
answer:
• Strong — the normal kind; never collected while reachable.
  • Soft — collected only when memory is tight. Good for caches.
  • Weak — collected at the next GC. Used by WeakHashMap for metadata keyed on objects.
  • Phantom — for cleanup actions after finalization.
tip: WeakHashMap entries vanish once the key has no other reference — perfect for caches you don't want to leak.
code:
WeakReference<Image> ref = new WeakReference<>(img);
Image i = ref.get();   // may be null — always check

question: Which JVM flags matter in production?
answer:
• -Xms / -Xmx — initial/max heap. Set them equal in production to avoid resize pauses.
  • -XX:+UseG1GC, -XX:MaxGCPauseMillis — collector and pause target.
  • -XX:+HeapDumpOnOutOfMemoryError — essential for diagnosing OOM.
  • -XX:MaxRAMPercentage — the right way to size a heap in a container.
tip: In containers use -XX:MaxRAMPercentage rather than a fixed -Xmx, so the JVM adapts to the container's memory limit.
code:
java -Xms2g -Xmx2g -XX:+UseG1GC \
     -XX:+HeapDumpOnOutOfMemoryError -jar app.jar

question: How do you diagnose a slow or leaking Java application?
answer:
• jstack — thread dump; finds deadlocks and stuck threads.
  • jmap + Eclipse MAT — heap dump; finds what's retaining memory.
  • jstat — live GC statistics.
  • JFR + JDK Mission Control — low-overhead production profiling.
  • JMH — trustworthy microbenchmarks.
tip: Java Flight Recorder is designed for production — a few percent overhead, so you can profile the real system, not a guess.
code:
jstack <pid>                 # thread dump
jmap -dump:live,format=b,file=heap.hprof <pid>
jstat -gcutil <pid> 1000     # GC every second

question: What are the SOLID principles?
answer:
• Single responsibility — one reason to change.
  • Open/closed — open for extension, closed for modification.
  • Liskov substitution — a subtype must work anywhere its parent does.
  • Interface segregation — many small interfaces beat one fat one.
  • Dependency inversion — depend on abstractions, not concretions.
tip: Have one concrete example ready per principle — reciting the acronym without examples is an obvious red flag.
deep:
S — Single Responsibility

A class should have one reason to change. A UserService that validates, saves to the DB, and sends email has three: validation rules, schema changes, and the mail provider. Split it.

O — Open/Closed

// Closed to extension — every new type edits this method
double area(Shape s) {
  if (s instanceof Circle) ...
  else if (s instanceof Square) ...
}

// Open — a new shape adds a class, changes nothing
interface Shape { double area(); }

L — Liskov Substitution

The classic violation: Square extends Rectangle. Setting width on a Square must also change height, so code written against Rectangle (set width to 5, height to 4, expect area 20) silently breaks. The inheritance is mathematically true but behaviourally wrong.

Warning sign: a subclass overriding a method to throw UnsupportedOperationException.

I — Interface Segregation

// Fat — a Robot must implement eat()
interface Worker { void work(); void eat(); }

// Segregated
interface Workable { void work(); }
interface Feedable { void eat(); }

D — Dependency Inversion

// Depends on a concretion — untestable
class OrderService { private final MySqlRepo repo = new MySqlRepo(); }

// Depends on an abstraction — inject it, mock it in tests
class OrderService {
  private final OrderRepository repo;
  OrderService(OrderRepository repo) { this.repo = repo; }
}

This is exactly what Spring's constructor injection gives you — and why DI makes code testable.

How to say it in an interview

“They're guidelines for change-tolerant code. The two I lean on daily are dependency inversion — constructor-inject an interface so I can mock it in tests — and single responsibility. The one people get wrong is Liskov: Square extends Rectangle compiles fine but breaks every caller's expectations.”

question: How do you implement a Singleton correctly?
answer:
Best: an enum — the JVM guarantees one instance, and it's serialization- and reflection-safe for free.

Otherwise use the holder idiom: a static nested class loaded lazily on first access, made thread-safe by the classloader with no synchronization.
tip: Enum singleton is Josh Bloch's recommendation — it's the only approach reflection can't break.

question: What is the Factory pattern?
answer:
It moves object creation behind a method, so callers ask for what they need without knowing which concrete class they get. Adding a new type doesn't touch calling code.

The JDK is full of them: List.of(), Integer.valueOf(), Executors.newFixedThreadPool().
tip: A static factory also beats a constructor because it can have a meaningful name, return a cached instance, and return a subtype.

question: What is the Builder pattern and when do you use it?
answer:
It builds an object step by step through a fluent chain — the fix for the telescoping constructor problem, where many optional parameters produce a dozen overloads and unreadable call sites.

It also makes the result immutable and lets you validate in build().
tip: Four-plus constructor parameters — especially several of the same type — is the signal to reach for a builder. new User(true, false, true) is unreadable.
code:
User u = User.builder()
    .name("Ann")
    .email("a@x.com")
    .active(true)
    .build();

question: What is the Strategy pattern?
answer:
It puts each algorithm behind a common interface so it can be swapped at runtime — replacing a growing if/else or switch over behaviour types.

In modern Java the strategy is usually just a lambda: Comparator passed to sort() is Strategy.
tip: Every lambda you pass into a method is Strategy. Since Java 8 the pattern mostly stopped needing its own classes.

question: What is the Observer pattern?
answer:
A subject keeps a list of listeners and notifies them all when its state changes — so publishers and subscribers stay decoupled.

It's everywhere: Swing listeners, Spring's ApplicationEvent, RxJava, and any pub/sub system.
tip: Its classic bug is the lister leak: an observer that never unregisters keeps the whole subscriber graph reachable forever.
code:
private final List<Listener> listeners = new CopyOnWriteArrayList<>();
void publish(Event e) { listeners.forEach(l -> l.onEvent(e)); }

question: What is the Decorator pattern?
answer:
It wraps an object in another with the same interface to add behaviour without subclassing — and the wrappers compose in any order.

The canonical example is java.io: new BufferedReader(new InputStreamReader(in)).
tip: Decorator adds behaviour with the same interface; Proxy controls ACCESS to it. That distinction is a common follow-up.

question: What is dependency injection and why does it matter?
answer:
A class receives its collaborators from outside instead of constructing them (new). That inverts control: the class depends on an interface, and the caller (or a container like Spring) decides the implementation.

The payoff is testability — you can pass a mock in a unit test.
tip: Prefer constructor injection: dependencies can be final, the object is never half-built, and it works without a framework in tests.

question: Why favour composition over inheritance?
answer:
Inheritance is the tightest coupling in OOP: a subclass depends on its parent's implementation, so a parent change silently breaks it (the fragile base class problem). It's also fixed at compile time and single.

Composition — holding a collaborator and delegating — is swappable at runtime and unaffected by the other class's internals.
tip: Use inheritance only for a genuine is-a with a class designed for it (documented, or final). Otherwise compose.

question: How do you structure a typical layered Java backend?
answer:
• Controller — HTTP only: parse, validate, map to DTOs. No business logic.
  • Service — business rules and transaction boundaries.
  • Repository — persistence only.

Each layer depends on the one below through an interface, and entities don't leak out as API responses — map to DTOs.
tip: Returning JPA entities straight from a controller leaks your schema into your API and causes lazy-loading errors. Map to a DTO.

question: Why is Java not 100% object-oriented?
answer:
Because of primitives — int, double, boolean etc. are not objects. Java also has static members that belong to no instance, and operators that aren't method calls.

Wrapper classes and autoboxing paper over the gap, but the primitives themselves remain non-objects (a deliberate performance choice).
tip: Contrast with Smalltalk/Ruby where literally everything is an object. Primitives are the one-word answer.

question: What changed in interfaces in Java 8 (default/static) and Java 9 (private methods)?
answer:
Java 8: interfaces can have default methods (implementation inherited by implementors — lets you evolve an API without breaking every implementor, e.g. List.sort) and static methods (utility helpers called on the interface itself).

Java 9: private (and private static) methods — shared helper code between default methods without exposing it.

Fields are still implicitly public static final; there's no state, so interfaces still aren't abstract classes.
tip: Follow-up is always the diamond problem: two interfaces with the same default method force the class to override it (and it can pick one via InterfaceName.super.method()).
deep:
Why default methods exist

Java 8 added stream() to Collection. Without default methods, every collection implementation on the planet would have failed to compile. Default methods = binary-compatible API evolution.

Resolution rules (the interview meat)

  1. Class wins — a concrete/abstract method in the class hierarchy always beats an interface default.
  2. Most specific interface wins — if B extends A and both have the default, B's is used.
  3. Otherwise: compile error — the implementing class must override and may delegate with A.super.method().

Interface vs abstract class after Java 8

The line has blurred, but abstract classes still uniquely offer: instance state (fields), constructors, non-public members, and final methods. Interfaces uniquely offer multiple inheritance of type.

Java 9 private methods

interface Retryable {
  default void retryFast()  { retry(3, 100); }
  default void retrySlow()  { retry(5, 1000); }
  private void retry(int times, long delayMs) { /* shared logic */ }
}

question: Can an abstract class have a constructor? Can it be instantiated?
answer:
Yes, it can have a constructor; no, it cannot be instantiated directly with new.

The constructor runs via constructor chaining when a concrete subclass is instantiated — it initializes the fields the abstract class owns. Anonymous subclasses (new AbstractType() { ... }) are still subclasses, not direct instantiation.
tip: An abstract class can even have all methods implemented (zero abstract methods) — declaring it abstract just forbids instantiation.

question: What is a covariant return type?
answer:
Since Java 5, an overriding method may declare a narrower (subclass) return type than the method it overrides — that's a covariant return type.

It removes casts for callers: Dog.reproduce() can return Dog even though Animal.reproduce() returns Animal. It only works for reference types, never primitives (long → int is illegal).
tip: Classic real example: Object.clone() returns Object, but your override can return your own type.

question: What is a marker interface? Examples?
answer:
An interface with no methods — it only tags a class with a capability that the runtime or a framework checks via instanceof.

Examples: Serializable, Cloneable, RandomAccess. ObjectOutputStream throws NotSerializableException for non-Serializable objects; Object.clone() throws for non-Cloneable ones.
tip: Follow-up: annotations largely replaced marker interfaces — but an interface participates in the type system (you can write a method that only accepts Serializable), an annotation can't.

question: Can you override a static method? A private method? A final method?
answer:
No to all three — but for different reasons:

  • static — redeclaring it in a subclass is method hiding, not overriding: which one runs depends on the reference type, not the object. No polymorphism.
  • private — invisible to the subclass; a same-signature method is simply a brand-new, unrelated method.
  • final — compile error; that's the whole point of final.
tip: The trap question: Parent p = new Child(); p.staticMethod() calls Parent's version — static dispatch is by reference type.

question: throw vs throws?
answer:
throw is a statement that actually raises one exception instance: throw new IllegalStateException(...).

throws is a method-signature declaration listing checked exceptions the method may propagate, shifting handling responsibility to the caller.
tip: throw = do it (one object, inside the body). throws = declare it (a list of types, in the signature).

question: What is exception propagation?
answer:
When an exception isn't caught in the method where it's thrown, it bubbles up the call stack frame by frame until a matching catch is found. If it reaches the top, the thread dies and the default handler prints the stack trace.

Unchecked exceptions propagate silently; checked exceptions propagate only if every method on the way declares them in throws.
tip: The stack trace reads bottom-up as the propagation path — deepest frame (the throw site) is at the top.
code:
void a() { b(); }            // no handler -> propagates out of a()
void b() { c(); }            // no handler -> propagates out of b()
void c() { int x = 1 / 0; }  // ArithmeticException starts here

question: What are the rules for exceptions when overriding a method?
answer:
An override may throw:

  • the same checked exceptions, narrower (subclass) ones, fewer, or none at all;
  • any unchecked exceptions — they're never part of the contract.

It may not throw new or broader checked exceptions — callers coded against the parent type would face exceptions they never had to handle.

Related override rules: access modifier can only widen (protected → public), return type may be covariant.
tip: Same logic as Liskov substitution: the override can't be more demanding on callers than the parent's contract.

question: final vs finally vs finalize()?
answer:
Three unrelated things that only sound alike:

  • final — keyword: constant variable, non-overridable method, non-extendable class.
  • finally — block after try/catch that runs regardless of exceptions; used for cleanup.
  • finalize() — a method the GC might call before reclaiming an object. Unpredictable, slow, deprecated since Java 9 and removed for good reasons — never rely on it; use try-with-resources or Cleaner.
tip: This is a rapid-fire trap — answer in three short lines, and volunteer that finalize() is deprecated.

question: IdentityHashMap, WeakHashMap, EnumMap — what are they for?
answer:
• IdentityHashMap — compares keys with == instead of equals(). Used by frameworks for object-graph traversal (serialization, deep-copy) where two equal-but-distinct objects must stay separate.
  • WeakHashMap — keys are held by weak references; once a key has no strong references elsewhere, GC removes the entry. Classic for memory-sensitive caches keyed by objects you don't own.
  • EnumMap — keys are a single enum type, backed by a plain array indexed by ordinal. Faster and leaner than HashMap; iteration follows enum declaration order.
tip: One-liner each: identity = == keys, weak = GC evicts entries, enum = array-backed and fastest for enum keys.
code: Map<Status, Handler> handlers = new EnumMap<>(Status.class); // not HashMap

question: Iterator vs ListIterator vs Enumeration?
answer:
• Iterator — universal (any Collection), forward-only, supports remove(), fail-fast.
  • ListIterator — lists only; adds bidirectional traversal (hasPrevious/previous), index access, and set()/add() during iteration.
  • Enumeration — legacy (Vector/Hashtable), forward-only, no remove, not fail-fast. Only met in old APIs; prefer Iterator.
tip: ListIterator is the only one that can modify elements in place and walk backwards.
code:
ListIterator<String> it = list.listIterator(list.size());
while (it.hasPrevious()) System.out.println(it.previous()); // reverse walk

question: Vector vs ArrayList?
answer:
Both are resizable arrays. Vector (Java 1.0) synchronizes every method and grows by 100%; ArrayList is unsynchronized and grows by 50%.

Vector's per-method locking is both slow and insufficient (iteration still needs external locking), so it's effectively deprecated. Need thread safety? Use CopyOnWriteArrayList or Collections.synchronizedList().
tip: Same story as Stack vs ArrayDeque and Hashtable vs ConcurrentHashMap — the legacy synchronized classes all have modern replacements.

question: findFirst vs findAny? anyMatch/allMatch/noneMatch? What are short-circuit operations?
answer:
findFirst() returns the first element in encounter order; findAny() returns whichever is found first — identical for sequential streams, but findAny is faster on parallel streams because it doesn't coordinate ordering. Both return Optional.

anyMatch / allMatch / noneMatch take a Predicate and return boolean.

Short-circuit operations stop processing as soon as the result is known: findFirst/findAny, the three matchers, limit(), takeWhile() — an infinite stream + limit still terminates.
tip: anyMatch(p) short-circuits on the first true; allMatch(p) on the first false. On an empty stream allMatch and noneMatch are vacuously true.
code:
boolean hasAdmin = users.stream().anyMatch(u -> u.role() == ADMIN); // stops at first hit
Optional<User> any = users.parallelStream().findAny();              // no order penalty

question: What is the difference between peek() and map()?
answer:
map() transforms — takes a Function, replaces each element with its result. peek() observes — takes a Consumer, passes each element through unchanged.

peek() is intended for debug logging mid-pipeline. Don't use it for real side effects: it only runs when a terminal operation pulls elements, and the JVM may skip it entirely when it can prove the result doesn't need it (e.g. count()).
tip: Gotcha: stream.peek(...) with no terminal operation executes nothing — streams are lazy.
code:
List<String> out = names.stream()
    .peek(n -> log.debug("raw: {}", n))   // observe
    .map(String::toUpperCase)              // transform
    .toList();

question: How do you sort in a stream with a Comparator? (employees by salary desc, then name)
answer:
sorted() uses natural ordering (elements must be Comparable); sorted(Comparator) takes any ordering. Build comparators declaratively with Comparator.comparing + reversed() / thenComparing.

sorted() is a stateful intermediate operation — it must buffer the whole stream before emitting anything.
tip: comparingInt/comparingLong/comparingDouble avoid boxing. Watch reversed() placement — it reverses everything before it, so put it right after the key it applies to.
code:
List<Employee> sorted = employees.stream()
    .sorted(Comparator.comparingDouble(Employee::salary).reversed()
        .thenComparing(Employee::name))
    .toList();

question: Process vs thread?
answer:
A process is a running program with its own memory space; a thread is an execution path inside a process, sharing its heap with sibling threads while keeping a private stack, program counter and registers.

  • Processes are isolated — one crashing doesn't kill another; threads share state, so one bad thread can corrupt or crash the whole process.
  • Thread creation/context-switching is much cheaper; inter-thread communication is just shared memory (which is exactly why synchronization is needed).
tip: One JVM = one process; every Thread you start lives inside it and shares the same heap.

question: start() vs run()? What if you call start() twice?
answer:
start() asks the JVM to create a new OS thread which then calls run(). Calling run() directly is just a normal method call — everything executes on the current thread, no concurrency at all.

Calling start() twice throws IllegalThreadStateException — a thread that has left NEW can never be restarted; create a new Thread instead.
tip: Classic trap: t.run() prints on the main thread. Check with Thread.currentThread().getName().

question: sleep() vs wait() vs yield() vs join()?
answer:
• sleep(ms) — static; pauses the current thread for a fixed time, keeps its locks.
  • wait() — on an object; releases that object's lock and parks until notify/notifyAll; must be inside synchronized.
  • yield() — a hint to the scheduler to let equal-priority threads run; may do nothing, no lock release. Rarely useful outside benchmarks.
  • join() — current thread blocks until the target thread finishes; the standard "wait for the worker before reading its result".
tip: The two axes interviewers probe: which class owns the method (Thread vs Object) and who releases locks (only wait does).

question: synchronized method vs block vs static synchronized — and object-level vs class-level locks?
answer:
Every object has an intrinsic monitor; so does every Class object.

  • synchronized instance method — locks this (object-level lock). All synchronized instance methods of the same object exclude each other.
  • synchronized block — locks any object you choose, for as few lines as possible: less contention, and you can use a private lock object outsiders can't touch.
  • static synchronized — locks MyClass.class (class-level lock), shared across all instances.

Object-level and class-level locks are independent: a static and an instance synchronized method can run at the same time.
tip: Prefer a private final lock object over synchronized(this) — external code could lock your public 'this' and deadlock you.

question: What are livelock and starvation?
answer:
Both are liveness failures where threads are alive but no useful work happens:

  • Livelock — threads keep actively responding to each other without progressing (two threads each releasing and retrying a lock in the same rhythm, like two people side-stepping in a corridor forever). CPU is busy, nothing completes. Fix: add randomized backoff/jitter to retries.
  • Starvation — a thread never gets the resource because others always win: greedy high-priority threads, or an unfair lock always granted to newer requesters. Fix: fair locks (new ReentrantLock(true)), bounded queues, avoid priority abuse.
tip: Deadlock = frozen holding locks; livelock = busy but circular; starvation = one victim never scheduled.

question: Explain ThreadPoolExecutor's parameters (core size, max size, queue, rejection policies).
answer:
ThreadPoolExecutor(corePoolSize, maximumPoolSize, keepAliveTime, unit, workQueue, handler). On submit:

  1. Fewer than corePoolSize threads → create a new thread even if others are idle.
  2. Core full → queue the task.
  3. Queue full → grow up to maximumPoolSize.
  4. Max reached and queue full → the RejectedExecutionHandler fires.

Rejection policies: AbortPolicy (default, throws), CallerRunsPolicy (caller executes it — natural backpressure), DiscardPolicy, DiscardOldestPolicy.
tip: The counter-intuitive bit interviewers test: with an unbounded LinkedBlockingQueue, maximumPoolSize never matters — the queue never fills.
deep:
Sizing rules of thumb

  • CPU-bound: threads ≈ number of cores (Runtime.getRuntime().availableProcessors()).
  • IO-bound: threads ≈ cores × (1 + wait/compute ratio) — threads mostly blocked can be far more numerous. (Or use virtual threads on Java 21.)

Why Executors factory methods are risky

  • newFixedThreadPool — unbounded queue → OOM under sustained overload.
  • newCachedThreadPool — unbounded threads (max = Integer.MAX_VALUE) → thread explosion under burst.

Production code (and Alibaba's style guide) prefers the explicit constructor with a bounded queue and a deliberate rejection policy.

Picking a queue

  • ArrayBlockingQueue(n) — bounded, predictable; the usual choice.
  • SynchronousQueue — no buffering, hand-off directly; pairs with a large max (cached pool style).
  • LinkedBlockingQueue — unbounded by default; use only with an explicit capacity.

CallerRunsPolicy as backpressure

When saturated, the submitting thread runs the task itself, which slows down the producer — a simple self-throttling mechanism for web handlers feeding a worker pool.

question: submit() vs execute()?
answer:
• execute(Runnable) — from Executor; fire-and-forget, returns void. An uncaught exception kills the worker thread and reaches its UncaughtExceptionHandler (you'll see the stack trace).
  • submit(Runnable/Callable) — from ExecutorService; returns a Future. Exceptions are captured inside the Future and only surface when you call get() (as ExecutionException).

The classic bug: submit() a task, never call get() — the exception vanishes silently.
tip: Need a result or a checked-exception path? submit + Callable. Pure side effect? execute — at least failures get logged.

question: What are daemon threads?
answer:
Background service threads that don't keep the JVM alive — the JVM exits as soon as the last non-daemon (user) thread finishes, killing daemons abruptly mid-work (finally blocks may never run).

Examples: the garbage collector, JIT compiler threads. Set with setDaemon(true) before start() — after start it throws. A thread inherits daemon status from its creator.
tip: Never do IO/cleanup work you care about on a daemon thread — it can be killed at any instruction.

question: What is the fork/join framework?
answer:
A Java 7 framework for divide-and-conquer parallelism: a task recursively forks into subtasks until small enough, computes, then joins results. Implemented by ForkJoinPool with RecursiveTask<V> (returns a value) / RecursiveAction (void).

Its key idea is work stealing: each worker has its own deque; idle workers steal tasks from the tail of busy workers' deques, keeping all cores busy without a central bottleneck.

parallelStream() and CompletableFuture run on the shared ForkJoinPool.commonPool() by default.
tip: Awareness answer: divide-and-conquer + work stealing + it's what parallel streams use underneath. That's usually enough.

question: What are the JVM runtime memory areas (heap, stack, metaspace, PC register, native stack)?
answer:
• Heap — all objects and arrays; shared by all threads; GC-managed; sized by -Xms/-Xmx.
  • Java stacks — one per thread; a frame per method call holding locals, operand stack, return address. Overflow → StackOverflowError.
  • Metaspace — class metadata (bytecode, method tables); native memory, replaced PermGen in Java 8.
  • PC register — per thread, the address of the current bytecode instruction.
  • Native method stacks — for JNI/C code.

Also: the code cache (JIT-compiled machine code) and direct/off-heap buffers (NIO).
tip: Heap and metaspace are shared; stacks and PC registers are per-thread. That split is what the question is really testing.
deep:
What lives where — the tricky cases

  • Local primitives and references → stack; the objects they point to → heap.
  • Instance fields → inside their object on the heap (even primitive fields).
  • static fields → with class metadata; since Java 8 they live in the heap (alongside the java.lang.Class object), not metaspace — a common trivia trap.
  • String literals → the string pool, in the heap since Java 7.

Errors mapped to areas

  • OutOfMemoryError: Java heap space → heap exhausted (-Xmx).
  • OutOfMemoryError: Metaspace → classloader leak or unbounded proxy generation (-XX:MaxMetaspaceSize).
  • StackOverflowError → runaway recursion (-Xss per-thread stack size).
  • OOM: unable to create native thread → too many threads for OS memory.

Escape analysis footnote

The JIT can prove some objects never escape a method and allocate them on the stack (scalar replacement) — so "all objects on the heap" is the spec answer, with a real-world asterisk worth mentioning to impress.

question: PermGen vs Metaspace?
answer:
Both store class metadata; Java 8 replaced PermGen with Metaspace.

  • PermGen (≤ Java 7) — a fixed-size region inside the JVM heap; a hardcoded default (~64–85 MB) made OutOfMemoryError: PermGen space a routine failure on app servers with many redeploys.
  • Metaspace (Java 8+) — native memory, grows dynamically by default (cap with -XX:MaxMetaspaceSize). Class metadata is freed when its classloader is collected.

Classloader leaks still cause Metaspace OOM — it just takes longer to hit.
tip: Interned strings and static fields moved out of PermGen into the heap already in Java 7 — Metaspace holds only class metadata.
